privacy_data_protection.description: The extent to which digital euro transactions and user data will be private, balancing user anonymity with anti-money laundering (AML) and combating the financing of terrorism (CFT) requirements.
privacy_data_protection.salience: Highly contested by civil society organisations, privacy advocates, and some MEPs demanding robust safeguards, against financial institutions and law enforcement agencies stressing the need for traceability to combat illicit activities.

role_of_intermediaries_and_disintermedia.description: Defining the roles and responsibilities of commercial banks and other payment service providers in distributing the digital euro, and mitigating risks of financial disintermediation from the traditional banking sector.
role_of_intermediaries_and_disintermedia.salience: Commercial banks lobby for their continued central role and against measures allowing the ECB to directly disintermediate them or leading to large-scale deposit outflows. Fintechs might lobby for a more open distribution model.

legal_tender_status_and_mandatory_accept.description: Whether the digital euro will be granted full legal tender status and if its acceptance by merchants and public bodies will be mandatory across the Eurozone, ensuring its wide usability.
legal_tender_status_and_mandatory_accept.salience: Merchant associations often oppose mandatory acceptance due to potential costs of new infrastructure and payment processing fees. Consumer groups and the ECB generally support it to ensure wide usability and accessibility as a public good.

offline_functionality_and_accessibility.description: The requirement and technical feasibility of providing robust offline payment capabilities for the digital euro, ensuring accessibility for all citizens, including vulnerable groups and those lacking internet access.
offline_functionality_and_accessibility.salience: Consumer organisations and social rights groups strongly advocate for robust offline functionality. Technical implementers (banks, tech companies) might raise concerns about security, cost, and complexity of implementation.

limits_on_holdings_and_remuneration.description: The proposed limits on the amount of digital euro individuals can hold and whether these holdings will be remunerated, impacting financial stability and the transmission of monetary policy.
limits_on_holdings_and_remuneration.salience: Commercial banks lobby for strict limits and no remuneration to prevent large-scale deposit flight from their accounts. The ECB seeks flexibility for monetary policy, while consumer groups might advocate for higher limits or potential remuneration.

cost_recovery_and_funding_model.description: Determining how the costs for developing, operating, and maintaining the digital euro infrastructure and services will be recovered and funded across the ecosystem.
cost_recovery_and_funding_model.salience: Commercial banks and payment service providers will want to avoid bearing the full burden of infrastructure and service costs without appropriate compensation or revenue streams. Member States and the ECB will debate public funding vs. user or intermediary fees.

innovation_and_competition_in_payments.description: The impact of the digital euro on private sector innovation and competition within the European payments market, particularly regarding new payment solutions and business models.
innovation_and_competition_in_payments.salience: Fintechs and some technology companies might argue the digital euro could stifle private innovation if it's too dominant or restrictive. Established payment providers might see it as a threat or an opportunity depending on their integration strategy. Policymakers aim for a balance.

governance_and_oversight.description: The framework for the governance, supervision, and democratic accountability regarding the design, operation, and evolution of the digital euro by EU institutions and Member States.
governance_and_oversight.salience: Member States and the European Parliament will likely seek to ensure appropriate democratic oversight and accountability of the ECB's role, balancing its independence with public scrutiny over a critical public infrastructure.

interoperability_and_cross_border_paymen.description: Ensuring the digital euro's interoperability with existing national and international payment systems and its potential use for efficient cross-border transactions and international cooperation.
interoperability_and_cross_border_paymen.salience: Financial institutions and businesses involved in cross-border trade will lobby for seamless integration and international standards. The ECB might seek to enhance the euro's international role through robust cross-border functionality, while other countries might have concerns about financial sovereignty.

privacy_data_protection.description: The extent to which digital euro transactions and user data will be private, balancing user anonymity with anti-money laundering (AML) and combating the financing of terrorism (CFT) requirements.

commission_evidence.legislation_summary: The proposed legislation establishes the digital euro as a retail central bank digital currency (CBDC) designed to supplement physical cash and ensure the euro remains viable as legal tender in a fully digital context. It would be offered as a public digital payment instrument alongside existing private payment solutions, with the explicit aim of strengthening competition and innovation in the European retail payments market rather than replacing private alternatives. The proposal mandates that the digital euro support pan-European, interoperable payment solutions including full rollout of instant payments. Key requirements include ensuring high privacy standards for digital transactions, maintaining financial stability, and promoting accessibility and financial inclusion for all EU citizens.

commission_evidence.legislation_summary: The proposed regulation integrates the digital euro into the existing EU payments framework by extending the definition of "funds" to cover all forms of central bank money issued for retail use, including central bank digital currency. It mandates that online digital euro transactions follow the same data protection, privacy, and AML/CFT rules as private digital payment means, in conformity with PSD2, GDPR, and the EU AML/CFT framework, while providing enhanced privacy protections for offline, cash-like proximity payments. Full anonymity is explicitly excluded to preserve consistency with the Union's risk-based AML approach. The regulation also requires that digital euro users have access to the same onboarding and strong customer authentication functionalities offered through the EU-wide European Digital Identity Wallet, as required under Article 97 of PSD2. Finally, the proposal is positioned as part of the Commission's broader strategy for EU open strategic autonomy and strengthening the international role of the euro, ensuring the digital euro is interoperable with existing national and international payment systems.

EN                                                           1                                                                EN
      The objective of this proposal is to ensure that central bank money with the status of legal
     tender remains available to the general public, while offering a state-of-the-art and cost-
     efficient payment means, ensuring a high level of privacy in digital payments, maintaining
     financial stability and promoting accessibility and financial inclusion. For this purpose, the
     proposal establishes the digital euro that may be issued by the European Central Bank and
     national central banks of the Member States whose currency is the euro, as part of the
     Eurosystem, and provides the necessary regulatory framework that should ensure the effective
     use of the digital euro as a single currency throughout the euro area, meeting users’ needs in
     the digital age and fostering competition, efficiency, innovation and resilience in the EU’s
     digitalizing economy. The digital euro would not be programmable money and could
     therefore not be used to limits its spending to or direct it at specific goods or services: as a
     digital form of the single currency, it should be fully fungible.

commission_evidence.legislation_summary: The proposed legislation establishes the digital euro as a form of central bank money issued by the ECB and national central banks within the Eurosystem, aimed at preserving legal tender status in digital payments while ensuring privacy, financial stability, and financial inclusion. Although the excerpt provided does not specify concrete numerical holding limits or explicit remuneration rules, the broader regulatory framework is designed to safeguard financial stability — implying that holding limits will be imposed to prevent large-scale substitution of commercial bank deposits, which could disrupt monetary policy transmission. The digital euro is explicitly defined as non-programmable and fully fungible, meaning it cannot be restricted to specific uses or directed toward particular goods and services. The legal basis draws on Articles 127–133 TFEU, situating the digital euro firmly within the ECB's monetary policy mandate and ensuring any holding or remuneration rules serve macroprudential objectives rather than commercial ones.

commission_evidence.theme_description: Whether the digital euro will be granted full legal tender status and if its acceptance by merchants and public bodies will be mandatory across the Eurozone, ensuring its wide usability.
commission_evidence.legislation_excerpt:
Banknotes and coins - which are the only current forms of central bank money with legal
     tender available to the general public (including people, public authorities and businesses) –
     alone cannot support the EU’s economy in the digital age. Their use in payments therefore
     diminishes as online purchases increase and payment habits of the general public shift
     towards the large variety of private digital means of payment offered in the EU. This puts at
     stake the desirable balance between central bank money and private digital means of payment.
     This trend could even be reinforced in the future, with the emergence of third country central
     bank digital currencies (CBDC) and stablecoins1 issued by private firms, which could
     challenge the role of the euro in payments, in the EU and outside.

The lack of a widely available and usable form of central bank money technologically adapted
     to the digital age could also diminish trust towards commercial bank money, and ultimately
     towards the euro itself. Trust towards commercial bank money relies on the possibility of
     depositors to convert at par their deposits into central bank money with legal tender, which
     currently is only available in the form of cash. Lacking a form of a central bank money that
     can be used in the digital economy and is convertible at par with commercial bank deposits
     may undermine the monetary anchor role of central bank money, weakening financial stability
     and monetary sovereignty in the EU.

In this context, over recent years, the issuance of a retail CBDC has gained significant
     attention and traction.2 Like cash, a retail CBDC would be an official form of central bank
     money directly accessible to the general public, endowed with the status of legal tender. It
     would thus adapt the official forms of the currency to technological development,
     complementing cash.

commission_evidence.legislation_summary: The proposed legislation establishes the rationale for granting the digital euro (a retail CBDC) full legal tender status, positioning it as the digital equivalent of cash. The core concern is that as physical cash use declines and private digital payment methods proliferate, the public's ability to access central bank money in a usable digital form is eroding — threatening the monetary anchor function that underpins trust in commercial bank deposits and the euro itself. To address this, the digital euro would be issued as an official form of central bank money directly accessible to the general public, carrying legal tender status just like banknotes and coins, thereby complementing rather than replacing cash. This implies that — consistent with legal tender obligations — merchants and public bodies across the Eurozone would be required to accept it, ensuring the digital euro functions as a universally usable means of payment rather than a voluntary option.

commission_evidence.legislation_summary: The proposal establishes a digital euro issued by the ECB and Eurosystem national central banks to ensure central bank money with legal tender status remains accessible to the public in a digitally usable form. Commercial banks and other payment service providers are positioned as distributors of the digital euro, maintaining their intermediary role rather than being bypassed by direct central bank-to-citizen issuance. To guard against financial disintermediation — where citizens might shift deposits from commercial banks to digital euro holdings — the framework is expected to include holding limits or other safeguards that preserve the stability of the traditional banking sector. The digital euro is explicitly designed as non-programmable and fully fungible, meaning it cannot be restricted to specific purposes or merchants, ensuring it functions as a genuine general-purpose currency equivalent to cash. The overarching goal is to strengthen the monetary anchor role of central bank money in the digital economy while maintaining financial stability, privacy, and inclusion across the euro area.

commission_evidence.legislation_summary: The proposal establishes a digital euro issued by the ECB and national central banks as legal tender for the general public, designed as a non-programmable, fully fungible form of the single currency. Privacy is a central design requirement: the digital euro must minimise personal data processing, and existing EU data protection law — specifically the GDPR and EUDPR — will apply to its distribution and use wherever personal data is handled. The framework explicitly aligns digital euro payments with Union data protection standards, meaning payment service providers will face legal constraints on what data they can collect and retain. At the same time, the proposal acknowledges the need to balance this privacy objective against AML/CFT obligations, which require some level of transaction monitoring and identity verification. The overall architecture is therefore intended to offer high privacy by default while preserving the compliance mechanisms required under EU anti-money laundering rules.

commission_evidence.legislation_summary: In preparation for its proposed regulation on the digital euro, the European Commission conducted a targeted consultation between April and June 2022, gathering input from a broad range of stakeholders including payment service providers, infrastructure providers, merchants, AML supervisors, financial intelligence units, the European Data Protection Board, and consumer organisations. The consultation findings indicated strong support among professional respondents for granting the digital euro legal tender status, with wide availability, easy onboarding, instant settlement, and the ability to pay anytime and to anyone identified as the most critical features. EU citizens broadly supported universal access to public money in digital form, including for unbanked populations, while favouring a digital euro that is fast, private, cost-free, and widely accessible. Most respondents also backed limiting availability primarily to citizens residing and doing business within the EU, and saw a widely available digital euro as supporting the EU's open strategic autonomy. These consultation outcomes were designed to directly inform the Commission's impact assessment underpinning the legislative proposal, embedding stakeholder input into the regulatory design process from the outset.

amendment_evidence.amendment_text: 1 b.Without prejudice to Directive (EU) 2015/2366, the payment service provider to which the provision of digital euro payment services is switched shall, prior to the switching, make available to the digital euro user a list of the digital euro payment services, other than basic services, that it offers and that the user currently receives and that are eligible for switching. The digital euro user shall explicitly select any such additional services they wish to switch and shall be informed in advance of any fees applicable to services other than the basic services.
